diagrams.title: Three I/O Methods Comparison
diagrams.description: A comparative diagram showing the three main I/O methods: Programmed I/O, Interrupt-driven I/O, and DMA. Each method is illustrated with CPU, I/O Device, and optionally Memory/ DMA Controller, with arrows showing data flow and control signals. This helps students understand the evolution from simple to sophisticated I/O handling.
diagrams.purpose: Explain the fundamental differences between programmed I/O, interrupt-driven I/O, and DMA, highlighting CPU involvement and efficiency

diagrams.title: Interrupt-Driven I/O Flow Diagram
diagrams.description: A flowchart-style diagram showing the sequence of events in interrupt-driven I/O: CPU executes main program → I/O device becomes ready → I/O device raises interrupt request → CPU completes current instruction → CPU acknowledges interrupt → CPU jumps to interrupt service routine → ISR processes data → CPU returns to main program. This visualizes the asynchronous nature of interrupts.
diagrams.purpose: Explain the interrupt mechanism and the flow of control between the main program and interrupt service routine

diagrams.title: DMA Data Transfer Operation
diagrams.description: A diagram showing Direct Memory Access operation: I/O device requests DMA transfer → DMA controller sends HOLD request to CPU → CPU acknowledges and releases bus control → DMA controller manages data transfer directly between I/O device and memory → DMA sends interrupt to CPU when complete → CPU resumes bus control. Shows how DMA offloads data transfer from CPU.
diagrams.purpose: Explain how DMA enables direct data transfer between I/O devices and memory without CPU intervention, improving system performance
